【釋名】時珍曰︰按王安石《字說》云︰松柏為百木之長。松猶公也，柏猶伯也。故松從公，柏從白。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰松脂生太山山谷。六月採。

頌曰︰松處處有之。其葉有兩鬣、五鬣、七鬣。歲久則實繁。中原雖有，不及塞上者佳好也。松脂以通明如薰陸香顆者為勝。

宗奭曰︰松黃一如蒲黃，但味差淡。松子多海東來，今關右亦有，但細小味薄也。

時珍曰︰松樹 修聳多節，其皮粗濃有鱗形，其葉後凋。二、三月抽蕤生花，長四、五寸，采其花蕊為松黃。結實狀如豬心，疊成鱗砌，秋老則子長鱗裂。然葉有二針、三針、五針之別。三針者為栝子松，五針者為松子松。其子大如柏子，惟遼海及雲南者，子大如巴豆可食，謂之海松子，詳見果部。

孫思邈云︰松脂以衡山者為良。衡山東五百里，滿谷所出者，與天下不同。

蘇軾云︰鎮定松脂亦良。《抱朴子》云︰凡老松皮內自然聚脂為第一，勝於鑿取及煮成者。其根下有傷處，不見日月者為陰脂，尤佳。老松余氣結為茯苓。千年松脂化為琥珀。《玉策記》云︰千年松樹四邊枝起，上杪不長如偃蓋。其精化為青牛、青羊、青犬、青人、伏龜，其壽皆千歲。

松脂

【氣味】苦、甘，溫，無毒。

權曰︰甘，平。

震亨曰︰松脂屬陽金

【發明】弘景曰︰松、柏皆有脂潤，凌冬不凋，理為佳物，服食多用，但人多輕忽之爾。

  "original_text": "【釋名】（音寢）。

時珍曰︰按︰范成大《桂海志》云︰凡木葉心皆一縱理，獨桂有兩道如圭形，故字從圭。

陸佃《埤雅》云︰桂猶圭也。宣導百藥，為之先聘通使，如執圭之使也。《爾雅》謂之梫者，能侵害他木也。故《呂氏春秋》云︰桂枝之下無雜木。《雷公炮炙論》云︰桂釘木根，其木即死，是也。桂即牡桂之濃而辛烈者，牡桂即桂之薄而味淡者，《別錄》不當重出，今倂為一，而分目於下。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰桂生桂陽，牡桂生南海山谷。二月、八月、十月采皮，陰乾。弘景曰︰南海即是廣州。《神農本經》惟有牡桂、菌桂。俗用牡桂，扁廣殊薄，皮黃，脂肉甚少，氣如木蘭，味亦類桂，不是知別樹，是桂之老宿者？菌桂正圓如竹，三重者良，俗中不見，惟以嫩枝破卷成圓者用之，非真菌桂也，並宜研訪。今俗又以半卷多脂者，單名為桂，入藥最多，是桂有三種矣。此桂廣州出者好；交州、桂州者，形段小而多脂肉，亦好；湘州、始興、桂陽縣者，即是小桂，不如廣州者。《經》云︰桂，葉如柏葉澤黑，皮黃心赤。

齊武帝時，湘州送樹，植芳林苑中。今東山有桂皮，氣粗相類，而葉乖異，亦能凌冬，恐是牡桂。人多呼為丹桂，正謂皮赤爾。北方重此，每食輒須之，蓋《禮》所云薑桂以為芬芳也。

恭曰︰桂惟有二種。陶氏引《經》云似柏葉，不知此言從何所出？又於《別錄》剩出桂條，為深誤也。單名桂者，即是牡桂，乃《爾雅》所謂「梫木桂」也。葉長尺許，花、子皆與菌桂同。大小枝皮俱名牡桂。但大枝皮，肉理粗虛如木而肉少味薄，名曰木桂，亦云大桂；不及小嫩枝皮，肉多而半卷，中必皺起，其味辛美，一名肉桂，亦名桂枝，一名桂心，出融州、桂州、交州甚良。其菌桂，葉似柿葉，中有縱紋三道，表裡無毛而光澤。肌理緊薄如竹，大枝、小枝皮俱是筒。其大枝無肉，老皮堅版，不能重卷，味極淡薄，不入藥用；小枝薄而卷及二、三重者良。或名筒桂，陶云小桂是也。今惟出韶州。

保昇曰︰桂有三種︰菌桂，

【氣味】甘、辛，大熱，有小毒。

權曰︰桂心，苦、辛，無毒。元素曰︰肉桂，氣熱，味大辛，純陽也。杲曰︰桂，辛，熱，有毒。陽中之陽，浮也。氣之薄者，桂枝也；氣之厚者，桂肉也。氣薄則發泄，桂枝上行而發表；氣厚則發熱，桂肉下行而補腎。此天地親上親下之道也。好古曰︰桂枝入足太陽經，桂心入手少陰經血分，桂肉入足少陰、太陰經血分。細薄者為枝為嫩，厚脂者為肉為老。去其皮與裡，當其中者為桂心。

《別錄》言︰有小毒，又云︰久服神仙不老。雖有小毒，亦從類化。與黃芩、黃連為使，小毒何施？與烏頭、附子為使，全取其熱性而已。與巴豆、硇砂、乾漆、穿山甲、水蛭等同用，則小毒化為大毒。與人參、麥門冬、甘草同用，則調中益氣，便可久服也。

之才曰︰桂得人參、甘草、麥門冬、大黃、黃芩，調中益氣。得柴胡、紫石英、乾地黃，療吐逆。忌生蔥、石脂。
苦、辛，無毒。詳前桂下。
辛，溫，無毒。權曰︰甘、辛。元素曰︰桂枝味辛、甘，氣微熱，氣味俱薄，體輕而上行，浮而升，陽也。余見前單桂下。

【主治】利肝肺氣，心腹寒熱、冷痰，霍亂轉筋、頭痛、腰痛、出汗，止煩，止唾，咳嗽，鼻齆，墮胎，溫中，堅筋骨，通血脈，理疏不足，宣導百藥，無所畏。久服神仙不老（《別錄》）。補下焦不足，治沉寒痼冷之病，滲泄，止渴，去營衛中風寒，表虛自汗。春夏為禁藥，秋冬下部腹痛，非此不能止（元素）。補命門不足，益火消陰（好古）。治寒痺風瘖，陰盛失血，瀉痢驚癇（時珍）。
九種心痛，腹內冷氣痛不可忍，咳逆結氣壅痺，腳痺不仁，止下痢，殺三蟲，治鼻中息肉，破血，通利月閉，胞衣不下（甄權）。治一切風氣，補五勞七傷，通九竅，利關節，益精明目，暖腰膝，治風痺骨節攣縮，續筋骨，生肌肉，消瘀血，破癖症瘕，殺草木毒（大明）。治風僻失音喉痺，陽虛失血，內托癰疽痘瘡，能引血化汗、化膿，解蛇蝮毒（時珍）。
上氣，咳逆，結氣，喉痺，吐吸，利關節，補中，益氣，久服通神，輕身不老（《本經》）。心痛、脅痛、脅風，溫筋，通脈，止煩，出汗（《別錄》）。去冷風疼痛（甄權）。去傷風頭痛，開腠理，解表發汗，去皮膚風濕（元素）。泄奔豚，散下焦畜血，利肺氣（成無己）。橫行手臂，治痛風（震亨）。
搗碎浸水，洗發，去垢除風（時珍）。

【發明】宗奭曰︰桂甘、辛，大熱。《素問》云︰辛甘發散為陽。故漢張仲景桂枝湯治傷寒表虛，皆須此藥，正合辛甘發散之意。本草三種之桂，不用牡桂、菌桂者，此二種性只於溫，不可以治風寒之病也。然《本經》只言桂，仲景又言桂枝者，取枝上皮也。

好古曰︰或問︰本草言桂能止煩出汗，而張仲景治傷寒有當發汗，凡數處，皆用

【釋名】筒桂（《唐本》）、小桂。

恭曰：箘者，竹名。此桂嫩而易卷如筒，即古所用筒桂也。筒似箘字，後人誤書為箘，習而成俗，亦復因循也。時珍曰︰今本草又作從草之菌，愈誤矣。牡桂為大桂，故此稱小桂。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰ 桂生交趾、桂林山谷岩崖間。無骨，正圓如竹，立秋采之。

弘景曰︰交趾屬交州，桂林屬廣州。《蜀都賦》云「 桂臨岩」是矣。

俗中不見正圓如竹者，惟嫩枝破捲成圓，猶依桂用，非真箘桂也。《仙經》用箘桂，云三重者良，則明非今桂矣。別是一物，應更研訪。

時珍曰︰ 桂，葉似柿葉者是。詳前桂下。《別錄》所謂正圓如竹者，謂皮卷如竹筒。

陶氏誤疑是木形如竹，反謂卷成圓者非真也。今人所栽岩桂，亦是 桂之類似柿葉，亦有鋸齒如枇杷葉而粗澀者，有無鋸齒如梔子葉而光潔者。叢生岩嶺間，謂之岩桂，俗呼為木犀。其花有白者名銀桂，黃者名金桂，紅者名丹桂。有秋花者，春花者，四季花者，逐月花者。其皮薄而不辣，不堪入藥。惟花可收茗、浸酒、鹽漬，及作香搽、發澤之類耳。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。
辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】百病，養精神，和顏色，為諸藥先聘通使。久服輕身不老，面生光華，媚好常如童子（《本經》）。

  "translation": "**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also known as: Tong Gui [Tube Cassia] (from the *Tang Materia Medica*); Xiao Gui [Small Cassia].

Su Gong states: The character *jun* (箘) is the name of a type of bamboo. The bark of this cassia, when young and tender, rolls easily into a tube shape, and this is the "tube cassia" [tong gui] used in antiquity. The character *tong* (筒) resembles *jun* (箘), and later generations mistakenly wrote one for the other; through long habit this error became accepted convention and was simply perpetuated. Li Shizhen remarks: In current materia medica texts the character is further corrupted to *jun* (菌) written with the grass radical, which is an even greater mistake. Since Mu Gui [Male Cassia] is called "large cassia," this one is accordingly called "small cassia."

**[Collected Commentaries]**
The *Mingyi Bielu* [Supplementary Records of Famous Physicians] states: Jun Gui grows among the mountain valleys and rocky cliffs of Jiaozhi and Guilin. It has no woody core [lit. "no bone"], is perfectly round like bamboo, and is harvested at the Beginning of Autumn [solar term].

Tao Hongjing states: Jiaozhi belongs to Jiao Prefecture; Guilin belongs to Guang Prefecture. The *Rhapsody on the Shu Capital*

results.title_inferred: Tianzhu Cinnamon (天竺桂) – Bark: Properties and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【集解】宗奭曰︰皮與牡桂相同，但薄耳。
時珍曰︰此即今閩、粵、浙中山桂也，而台州天竺最多，故名。大樹繁花，結實如蓮子狀。天竺僧人稱為月桂是矣。詳月桂下。

皮
【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。
【主治】腹內諸冷，血氣脹痛（藏器）。破產後惡血，治血痢腸風，補心同，方家少用。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations**

Kou Zongshi states: The bark is the same as that of mùguì [male/official cinnamon, *Cinnamomum cassia*], but thinner.

Li Shizhen states: This is the mountain cinnamon [shān guì] found today in Fujian, Guangdong, and Zhejiang. It grows most abundantly at Tianzhu in Taizhou, hence the name. It is a large tree with profuse flowers, bearing fruit shaped like lotus seeds [*Nelumbo* seeds]. Buddhist monks from Tianzhu [India] call it 'moon laurel' [yuè guì], which is indeed what it is. See the entry on Moon Laurel [月桂] for further detail.

**Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** All cold conditions within the abdomen; distension and pain due to blood and qi [藏器, Chen Cangqi]. Breaks up lochia [malign blood] after childbirth; treats bloody dysentery [xuè lì] and intestinal wind [cháng fēng]; tonifies the heart [bǔ xīn] similarly [to other cinnamons]. Seldom used by formulary physicians [fāng jiā].

entities_mentioned.name: 天竺桂 (Tiānzhú Guì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum japonicum Siebold (Japanese cinnamon / Tianzhu cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also identified by some scholars as Cinnamomum pedunculatum. Li Shizhen locates it in Fujian, Guangdong, and Zhejiang, and notes it as the 'mountain cinnamon' of those regions. The fruit is described as lotus-seed-shaped.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡桂 (Mùguì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Chinese cinnamon / cassia bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a reference standard; Tianzhu cinnamon bark is compared to it but described as thinner.

entities_mentioned.name: 月桂 (Yuèguì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Laurus nobilis or Cinnamomum japonicum (ambiguous; likely the same taxon or a close relative)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen states that Tianzhu Buddhist monks called this plant 'moon laurel.' He refers the reader to a separate entry on 月桂 for fuller discussion.

results.translator_notes: 1. The opening 【集解】曰︰天 in the source text is a clear OCR/truncation error; the character 天 and any preceding attribution are missing. Based on the immediately following attribution 宗奭曰, the fragment 曰︰天 has been silently dropped and the text begins with 宗奭曰 as the first complete statement. 2. The final attribution in 【主治】 is blank in the source (藏器 covers only the first clause; the second clause's attributed author is missing, represented by an empty parenthesis). This has been retained in the translation without fabricating an attribution. 3. '腸風' (intestinal wind) is a classical Chinese medical term for a type of rectal bleeding or bloody stool condition. 4. '補心同' likely means the bark tonifies the heart in a manner similar to other cinnamon varieties; the syntax is elliptical.

results.title_inferred: Moon Laurel (Yuegui) — Botanical Lore, Celestial Origins, and Medicinal Use
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰今江東諸處，每至四、五月後晦，多於衢路間得月桂子，大於狸豆，破之辛香，古者相傳是月中下也。余杭靈隱寺僧種得一株，近代詩人多所論述。《洞冥記》云︰有遠飛雞，朝往夕還，常銜桂實歸於南土。南土月路也，故北方無之。山桂猶堪為藥，況

時珍曰︰吳剛伐月桂之說，起於隋唐小說。月桂落子之說，起於武后之時。相傳有梵僧自天竺鷲嶺飛來，故八月常有桂子落於天竺。《唐書》亦云︰垂拱四年三月，有月桂子降於台州，十餘日乃止。宋仁宗天聖丁卯八月十五日夜，月明天淨，杭州靈隱寺月桂子降，其繁如雨，其大如豆，其圓如珠，其色有白者、黃者、黑者，殼如芡實，味辛。拾以進呈，寺僧種之，得二十五株，慈雲式公有序記之。張君房宿錢塘月輪寺，亦見桂子紛如煙霧，回旋成穗，墜如牽牛子，黃白相間，咀之無味。據此，則月中真若有樹矣。竊謂月乃陰魄，其中婆娑者，山河之影爾。月既無桂，則空中所墜者何物耶？泛觀群史，有雨塵沙土石，雨金鉛錢汞，雨絮帛谷粟，雨草木花藥，雨毛血魚肉之類甚眾。則桂子之雨，亦妖怪所致，非月中有桂也。桂生南方，故惟南方有之。《宋史》云︰元豐三年六月，饒州雨木子數畝，狀類山芋子，味辛而香。

子

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】小兒耳後月蝕瘡，研碎敷之（藏器）。
results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: In various places in Jiangdong, after the last day of the fourth or fifth month, seeds of the moon laurel [yuegui] are frequently found along roadsides. They are larger than fox beans [lǐdòu]; when broken open, they are pungently fragrant. It has been transmitted from antiquity that they fall from the moon. A monk at Lingyin Temple in Yuhang [near Hangzhou] cultivated one specimen, and many poets of recent times have written about it. The *Dongming Ji* [Record of Obscure Mysteries] states: There are birds called the Far-Flying Fowl [yuǎnfēi jī] that go out in the morning and return in the evening, and they regularly carry laurel fruits back to the southern lands. The southern lands are the domain of the moon's path, which is why [the moon laurel] is not found in the north. Even mountain laurel [shān guì] can serve as medicine, how much more so [the moon laurel] — [text appears to be truncated here].

Li Shizhen states: The legend of Wu Gang chopping the moon laurel originated in fiction [xiaoshuo] of the Sui and Tang dynasties. The story of moon laurel seeds falling from the sky originated in the time of Empress Wu [Wu Zetian]. It is said that a Brahmin monk flew over from Vulture Peak [Jiling] in India [Tianzhu], and therefore in the eighth month laurel seeds regularly fall at Tianzhu [a temple in Hangzhou, also sharing the name with India]. The *Tang Shu* [Book of Tang] also records: In the third month of the fourth year of the Chui Gong reign period [688 CE], moon laurel seeds descended upon Taizhou for more than ten days before stopping. On the night of the fifteenth day of the eighth month in the Tiansheng Dingmao year [1027 CE] of the reign of Emperor Renzong of Song, when the moon was bright and the sky clear, moon laurel seeds descended upon Lingyin Temple in Hangzhou — as plentiful as rain, as large as beans, as round as pearls, in colors of white, yellow, and black, with shells like those of Gordon euryale seeds [qiàn shí], and with a pungent flavor. They were gathered and presented [to the authorities], and the monks of the temple planted them, obtaining twenty-five saplings; the Venerable Ciyun Shi recorded the event in a preface. Zhang Junfang, spending the night at Yuelun Temple in Qiantang, also witnessed the laurel seeds falling like smoke and mist, swirling into clusters and dropping like seeds of morning glory [qiānniú zǐ], alternating yellow and white, but tasteless when chewed. Based on these accounts, it truly seems as though there is a tree on the moon. However, I venture to suggest that the moon is a body of yin essence [yīn pò], and what undulates within it is merely the reflection of mountains and rivers. Since the moon in fact contains no laurel, what then are the objects that fall from the sky? A broad survey of historical records shows numerous accounts of showers of dust, sand, earth, and stones; showers of gold, lead, coins, and mercury; showers of floss, silk, grain, and millet; showers of grasses, trees, flowers, and medicines; and showers of hair, blood, fish, and flesh — a great many such events. The rain of laurel seeds is therefore likewise caused by some uncanny [yāoguài] phenomenon, not by an actual laurel growing on the moon. The laurel [guì] grows in the south, which is why it is found only in the south. The *Song Shi* [History of the Song] records: In the sixth month of the third year of the Yuanfeng reign period [1080 CE], in Raozhou, tree seeds rained down over an area of several mu; they resembled the seeds of wild taro [shānyù zǐ] in appearance, and were pungently fragrant in flavor.

**Seeds**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Pungent; warm; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatment]** For moon-bite sores [yuè shí chuāng, a form of skin ulceration] behind the ears in children: grind [the seeds] to a powder and apply topically. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 月桂 (yuèguì, Moon Laurel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Osmanthus fragrans (sweet osmanthus) or possibly Cinnamomum camphora / a Lauraceae species; the 'moon laurel' of Chinese lore is distinct from Laurus nobilis but the seeds described suggest Osmanthus fragrans or a related species
entities_mentioned.notes: The passage is as much mythological as botanical. The seeds described (round, pungent, euryale-shell-like) are difficult to assign with certainty. Some scholars associate yuegui with Osmanthus fragrans, which blooms in the eighth month and whose seeds match some descriptions.

entities_mentioned.name: 山桂 (shān guì, Mountain Laurel/Cassia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia or a related Cinnamomum/Osmanthus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in passing as a medicinal variety.

entities_mentioned.name: 狸豆 (lǐ dòu, Fox Bean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mucuna pruriens (cowhage/velvet bean) or a related Mucuna species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size reference for the moon laurel seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: 芡實 (qiàn shí, Gordon Euryale seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euryale ferox
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for the shell of the moon laurel seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: 牽牛子 (qiānniú zǐ, Morning Glory seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea nil or Pharbitis nil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size and shape reference for the falling seeds observed by Zhang Junfang.

entities_mentioned.name: 吳剛 (Wu Gang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological figure said to eternally chop the moon laurel tree; Li Shizhen traces this legend to Sui–Tang fiction.

entities_mentioned.name: 月蝕瘡 (yuè shí chuāng, Moon-Bite Sores)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly aural eczema, seborrheic dermatitis, or a similar skin condition occurring behind the ear in children
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical dermatological term referring to sores that 'wax and wane' like the moon, typically behind the ears in children.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text for the 集解 section appears truncated mid-sentence after 況 ('how much more so'), likely due to a missing portion in the OCR source; this has been rendered as '[text appears to be truncated here]' in the translation.
2. Li Shizhen's skeptical rationalism is notable here: he explicitly rejects the supernatural explanation for the falling seeds, arguing they are anomalous atmospheric phenomena rather than lunar in origin.
3. 'Tianzhu' (天竺) is used in two senses: the Indian subcontinent and the Tianzhu Temple in Hangzhou — Li Shizhen deliberately exploits this ambiguity to explain the legend's origin.
4. 'Moon-bite sores' (月蝕瘡) is a classical pediatric dermatological term; the preparation method (grinding seeds to powder and applying topically) is attributed to Chen Cangqi (Zangqi), compiler of the *Bencao Shiyi* (c. 741 CE).
5. The Tiansheng Dingmao year corresponds to 1027 CE, the fifth year of the Tiansheng reign of Song Emperor Renzong.

【釋名】杜蘭（《別錄》）、林蘭（《本經》）

時珍曰︰其香如蘭，其花如蓮，故名。其木心黃，故曰黃心。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰木蘭生零陵山谷及太山。皮似桂而香。十二

弘景曰︰零陵諸處皆有之。狀如楠樹，皮甚薄而味辛香。今益州者皮濃，狀如濃朴，而氣味為勝。今東人皆以山桂皮當之，亦相類。道家用合香亦好。

Alternate names: Dulan [《Bielu》], Linlan [《Benjing》].

Li Shizhen states: Its fragrance resembles that of the orchid [lan], and its flower resembles the lotus [lian], hence the name [mulan]. Its heartwood is yellow, hence it is also called Huangxin ["yellow heart"].

**Collected Commentaries**

*《Bielu》* states: Mulan grows in the mountain valleys of Lingling and on Mount Tai. Its bark resembles that of cinnamon [gui] and is fragrant.

Tao Hongjing states: It is found throughout the Lingling region and elsewhere. It resembles the nan tree [Phoebe/Machilus] in form; its bark is very thin, with a pungent and fragrant taste. The variety from Yizhou today has thicker bark, resembling magnolia bark [houpo], and is superior in aroma and flavor. Easterners commonly substitute mountain cinnamon bark [shanguipi] for it, as the two are similar. Daoist practitioners also use it well in blended incense preparations.

Kou Zongshi [Baosheng] states: It is found everywhere. The tree reaches several ren [fathoms] in height. The leaves resemble those of jungui [Cinnamomum aromaticum], with three longitudinal veins, though the leaves are less pungently aromatic than cinnamon.

頌曰︰沉香、青桂等香，出海南諸國及交、廣、崖州。沈懷遠《南越志》云︰交趾蜜香樹，彼人取之，先斷其積年老木根，經年其外皮幹俱朽爛，木心與枝節不壞，堅黑沉水者，即沉香也。半浮半沉與水面平者，為雞骨香。細枝緊實未爛者，為青桂香。其幹為棧香。其根為黃熟香。其根節輕而大者，為馬蹄香。此六物同出一樹，有精粗之異爾，並采無時。劉恂《嶺表錄異》云︰廣管羅州多棧香樹，身似柜柳，其花白而繁，其葉如橘。其皮堪作紙，名香皮紙，灰白色，有紋如魚子，沾水即爛，不及楮紙，亦無香氣。沉香、雞骨、黃熟、棧香雖是一樹，而根、幹、枝、節，各有分別也。又丁謂《天香傳》云︰此香奇品最多。四香凡四名十二狀，出於一本。木體如白楊，葉如冬青而小。海北竇、化、高、雷皆出香之地，比海南者優劣不侔。既所稟不同，復售者多而取者速，其香不待稍成，乃趨利戕賊之深也。

非同瓊管黎人，非時不妄剪伐，故木無夭札之患，得必異香焉。宗奭曰︰嶺南諸郡悉有，傍海處尤多。交幹連枝，岡嶺相接，千里不絕。葉如冬青，大者數抱，木性虛柔。山民以構茅廬，或為橋梁，為飯甑。

【氣味】辛，微溫，無毒。曰︰苦，溫。大明曰︰辛，熱。元素曰︰陽也。有升有降。

【釋名】木蜜（《內典》）、沒香（《綱目》）
【集解】
藏器曰：蜜香生交州。大樹，節如沉香。《法華經》注云：木蜜，香蜜也。樹形似槐而香，伐之五、六年，乃取其香。《異物志》云：其葉如椿。樹生千歲，斫仆之，四、五歲乃往看，已腐敗，惟中節堅貞者是香。曰：生南海諸山中。種之五、六年便有香。《交州記》云：樹似沉香無異也。時珍曰：按《魏王花木志》云：木蜜號千歲樹，根本甚大，伐之四、五歲，取不腐者為香。觀此，則陳藏器所謂生千歲乃斫者，蓋誤訛也。段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：沒樹出波斯國、拂林國，人呼為阿[KT]。樹長丈餘，皮青白色，葉似槐而長，花似橘花而大。子黑色，大如山茱萸，酸甜可食。《廣州志》云：肇慶新興縣出多香木，俗名蜜香。辟惡氣，殺鬼精。《晉書》云：太康五年，大秦國獻蜜香樹皮紙，微褐色，有紋如魚子，極香而堅韌。觀此數說，則蜜香亦沉香之類，故形狀功用兩相仿佛。《南越志》謂：交人稱沉香為蜜香。《交州志》謂：蜜香似沉香。《嶺表錄異》言：棧香皮紙似魚子。尤可互証。楊慎《丹鉛錄》言蜜樹是蜜蒙花樹者，謬也。又枳木亦名木蜜，不知亦同類否？詳見果部。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】去臭，除鬼氣（藏器）。辟惡，去邪鬼尸注心氣。
results.translation:
**Names [Shìmíng]:** Mùmì [Wood-Honey] (cited in the *Nèidiǎn* [Buddhist canonical texts]); Mòxiāng [Lost/Absent Fragrance] (cited in the *Gāngmù*).

**Collected Explanations [Jíjiě]:**

Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Mìxiāng grows in Jiaozhou [modern northern Vietnam]. It is a large tree whose heartwood nodes resemble those of chen-xiang [agarwood/aloeswood]. The commentary on the *Lotus Sūtra* (*Fāhuājīng*) states: 'Mùmì is fragrant honey-wood. The tree's form resembles the Chinese scholar tree [huái, *Sophora japonica*] and is fragrant; one fells it and then, after five or six years, harvests the fragrance.' The *Yìwùzhì* [Record of Strange Things] states: 'Its leaves resemble those of the chūn tree [*Toona sinensis*]. The tree lives a thousand years; after it is felled, one returns four or five years later to find it rotted, and only the solid, unyielding heartwood nodes constitute the fragrance.'

[Source unnamed, possibly a cited authority:] It grows in the mountains of the South Seas. Once planted, it yields fragrance within five or six years. The *Jiāozhōu Jì* [Record of Jiaozhou] states: 'The tree is indistinguishable from chen-xiang.'

Li Shizhen [Shízhēn] states: I note that the *Wèi Wáng Huāmùzhì* [Prince of Wei's Flora] states: 'Mùmì is called the Thousand-Year Tree; its root and trunk are very large. After felling, one waits four or five years and then takes the non-rotted portions as fragrance.' In light of this, the claim by Chen Cangqi that the tree must live a thousand years before being felled is evidently an error. Duan Chengshi's *Yǒuyáng Záz'ǔ* [Miscellany of Youyang] states: 'The mò tree comes from Persia [Bōsī guó] and the kingdom of Fúlín [Byzantine Syria/Rome]; its people call it A-[KT] [ILLEGIBLE: transcription of a foreign-language name]. The tree grows to over a zhàng [≈3 m] in height; its bark is greenish-white; its leaves resemble those of the scholar tree but are longer; its flowers resemble orange blossoms but are larger; its fruits are black, about the size of cornelian cherry [shān zhūyú, *Cornus officinalis*], and are sour-sweet and edible.'

The *Guǎngzhōu Zhì* [Guangzhou Gazetteer] states: 'Zhàoqìng's Xīnxīng County produces much fragrant wood commonly called mìxiāng. It wards off noxious qi and destroys malevolent spirits.'

The *Jìnshū* [History of the Jin Dynasty] states: 'In the fifth year of the Tàikāng reign [284 CE], the kingdom of Dàqín [Rome/the Byzantine Empire] presented paper made from mìxiāng tree bark — slightly brownish in color, with a texture patterned like fish roe, extremely fragrant, and tough and pliable.'

Considering these various accounts, mìxiāng belongs to the same category as chen-xiang [agarwood], and accordingly their appearance and uses closely resemble one another. The *Nányuèzhì* [Record of Southern Yue] states that the people of Jiao call chen-xiang 'mìxiāng.' The *Jiāozhōu Zhì* states that mìxiāng resembles chen-xiang. The *Lǐngbiǎo Lùyì* [Record of the Wonders of the Southern Frontier] notes that the bark paper of zhàn-xiāng [a grade of agarwood] resembles fish roe. These sources mutually corroborate one another.

Yang Shen's [Yáng Shèn's] *Dānqiān Lù* [Red-Lead Record] claims that the mì tree is the mìméng flower tree [*Buddleja officinalis*] — this is mistaken. Furthermore, the zhǐ wood [*Hovenia dulcis*?] is also called mùmì; it is unknown whether this is the same category. See the Fruits section [Guǒ bù] for details.

**Qi and Flavor [Qìwèi]:** Acrid [xīn]; warm [wēn]; non-toxic.

**Indications [Zhǔzhì]:** Removes foul odors; dispels ghost qi [malevolent spiritual influences] (Chen Cangqi). Wards off evil; expels the qi of malevolent ghosts, corpse-reanimation afflictions [shī zhù], and heart qi [i.e., heart-related afflictions caused by such entities].

entities_mentioned.name: 蜜香 (Mìxiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Aquilaria sinensis or a closely related Aquilaria/Gonystylus species (agarwood family); possibly also confused with other fragrant tropical trees
entities_mentioned.notes: The text explicitly equates mìxiāng with chen-xiang (agarwood) or treats it as closely related. Multiple sources link it to Jiaozhou (Vietnam) and describe heartwood fragrance extracted from felled logs after years of decay — consistent with agarwood formation.

entities_mentioned.name: 沉香 (Chen-xiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria sinensis / Aquilaria malaccensis (agarwood/aloeswood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a reference comparison throughout the entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 槐 (Huái)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sophora japonica (Chinese scholar tree / Japanese pagoda tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf/form comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 椿 (Chūn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toona sinensis (Chinese mahogany / red toon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaf comparison cited in the Yìwùzhì.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Shān zhūyú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis (cornelian cherry / Japanese cornel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a fruit-size comparison for the mò tree fruit described in Youyang Zazhu.

entities_mentioned.name: 沒樹 (Mò shù) / 阿[KT]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a Persian or Syrian aromatic tree; the foreign-language name is illegible
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as coming from Persia and Fúlín (Byzantine Syria); the transliterated name '阿KT' is corrupted in the source and cannot be identified. The edible black fruits and large flowers do not clearly match Aquilaria species, suggesting possible confusion with another plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 蜜蒙花樹 (Mìméng huā shù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buddleja officinalis (butterfly bush / pale butterflybush)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly rejects Yang Shen's identification of mìxiāng with this plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 枳木 / 木蜜
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Hovenia dulcis (Japanese raisin tree) or Poncirus trifoliata; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen notes this plant also bears the name mùmì and directs readers to the Fruits section; the precise identification is left open.

results.translator_notes:
1. The segment contains a duplicated 'Collected Explanations' section in the source text (verbatim repetition); this appears to be a transcription/OCR artifact. The cleaned original text preserves it only once.
2. '阿KT' is clearly a garbled OCR rendering of a transliterated foreign word (likely Arabic, Persian, or Syriac); it is marked [ILLEGIBLE] in the translation. The original likely read something like '阿聒' or a similar phonetic transcription.
3. 'Fúlín' (拂林) is the Tang-period Chinese name for the Byzantine Empire or its Syrian/Levantine provinces, sometimes used more loosely for Rome.
4. The 'Tàikāng fifth year' (太康五年) corresponds to 284 CE, during the Western Jin dynasty.
5. The condition 'shī zhù' (尸注) refers to a classical Chinese medical concept of a contagious, wasting affliction associated with contact with corpses or malevolent spirits — not a modern diagnostic category.
6. The unnamed authority quoted (beginning '曰：生南海諸山中') likely refers to a cited source whose attribution was lost in transmission or OCR; it may be Chen Cangqi again or another materia medica author.
7. The entry's medical extraction data: Name — mìxiāng (honey fragrance wood); Modern ID — likely Aquilaria sp. (agarwood); Conditions treated — foul odors, malevolent spiritual qi, corpse-reanimation afflictions, heart qi disorders caused by evil entities; Preparation method — not explicitly stated in the preserved fragment; the wood/bark itself is used as fragrance and apparently burned or applied.

【釋名】丁子香（《嘉 》）、雞舌香。

藏器曰︰雞舌香與丁香同種，花實叢生，其中心最大者為雞舌（擊破有順理而解為兩向，如雞舌，故名），乃是母丁香也。禹錫曰︰按︰《齊民要術》云︰雞舌香，俗人以其似丁子，故呼為丁子香。

【集解】恭曰︰雞舌香樹葉及皮並似栗，花如梅花，子似棗核，此雌樹也，不入香用。

其雄樹雖花不實，采花釀之以成香。出昆侖及交州、愛州以南。 曰︰丁香生東海及昆侖國。

志曰︰丁香生交、廣、南番。按︰《廣州圖》上丁香，樹高丈餘，木類桂，葉似櫟葉。花圓細，黃色，凌冬不凋。其子出枝蕊上如釘，長三、四分，紫色。其中有粗大如山茱萸者，俗呼為母丁香。二月、八月採子及根。一云︰盛冬生花，子，至次年春采之。頌曰︰雞舌香，《唐本草》言其木似栗；《南越志》言是沉香花；《廣志》言是草花蔓香口。其說不定。今人皆以乳香中揀出木實似棗核者為之，堅頑枯燥，絕無氣味，燒亦無香，用療氣與口臭則甚乖疏，不知緣何以為雞舌也？京下老醫言︰雞舌與丁香同種，其中最大者為雞舌，即母丁香，療口臭最良，治氣亦效。葛稚川《百一方》︰治暴氣刺心痛，用雞舌香酒服。又《抱朴子》書︰以雞舌、黃連，乳汁煎之，注目，治百疹之在目者皆愈，

藏器曰：雞舌香與丁香同種，花實叢生，其中心最大者為雞舌（擊破有順理而解為兩向，如雞舌，故名），乃是母丁香也。禹錫曰：按：《齊民要術》云：雞舌香，俗人以其似丁子，故呼為丁子香。

【集解】恭曰：雞舌香樹葉及皮並似栗，花如梅花，子似棗核，此雌樹也，不入香用。其雄樹雖花不實，采花釀之以成香。出昆侖及交州、愛州以南。曰：丁香生東海及昆侖國。志曰：丁香生交、廣、南番。按：《廣州圖》上丁香，樹高丈餘，木類桂，葉似櫟葉。花圓細，黃色，凌冬不凋。其子出枝蕊上如釘，長三、四分，紫色。其中有粗大如山茱萸者，俗呼為母丁香。二月、八月採子及根。一云：盛冬生花，子，至次年春采之。頌曰：雞舌香，《唐本草》言其木似栗；《南越志》言是沉香花；《廣志》言是草花蔓香口。其說不定。今人皆以乳香中揀出木實似棗核者為之，堅頑枯燥，絕無氣味，燒亦無香，用療氣與口臭則甚乖疏，不知緣何以為雞舌也？京下老醫言：雞舌與丁香同種，其中最大者為雞舌，即母丁香，療口臭最良，治氣亦效。葛稚川《百一方》：治暴氣刺心痛，用雞舌香酒服。又《抱朴子》書：以雞舌、黃連，乳汁煎之，注目，治百疹之在目者皆愈，更加精明。古方治瘡癰五香連翹湯用雞舌香，而孫真人《千金方》無雞舌，用丁香，似為一物也。其采花釀成香之說，絕無知者。慎微曰：沈存中《筆談》云：予集《靈苑方》，據陳藏器《拾遺》，以雞舌為丁香母。今考之尚不然，雞舌即丁香也。《齊民要術》言雞舌俗名丁子香。《日華子》言丁香治口氣，與《三省故事》載漢時郎官日含雞舌香，欲其奏事芬芳之說相合。及《千金方》五香湯用丁香無雞舌，最為明驗。《開寶本草》重出丁香，謬矣。今世以乳香中大如山茱萸者為母丁香。

【氣味】辛，微溫，無毒。時珍曰：辛，溫。辛，溫，無毒。時好古曰：純陽。入手太陰、足少陰、陽明經。曰：方中多用雌者，力大。膏煎中若用雄，須去丁，蓋乳子發人背癰也。不可見火。畏郁金。辛，熱，有毒。

【主治】風水毒腫，霍亂心痛，去惡氣（《別錄》）。吹鼻，殺腦疳。入諸香中，令人身香（甄權）。同薑汁，塗拔去白須。丁香（《開寶》）溫脾胃，止霍亂擁脹，風毒諸腫，齒疳。能發諸香（《開寶》）。風疳骨槽勞臭，殺蟲辟惡去邪，治奶頭花，止五色毒痢、療五痔（李珣）。治口氣、冷氣、冷勞反胃、鬼疰、蠱毒，殺酒毒，消癖，療腎氣、奔豚氣、陰痛、腹痛，壯陽，暖腰膝（大明）。療嘔逆，甚驗（保升）。去胃寒，理元氣。氣血盛者勿服（元素）。治虛噦，小兒吐瀉，痘瘡胃虛，灰白不發，及齒痛（李珣）。心腹冷氣。

枝：一切冷氣，心腹脹滿，惡心，泄瀉虛滑，水穀不消（用枝杖七斤，肉豆蔻（面煨）八斤，白面（炒）六斤，甘草（炒）十一斤，炒鹽三斤，為末。日藥院方）。

根：風熱毒腫。不入心腹之用（《開寶》）。

【發明】好古曰：丁香與五味子、廣術同用，治奔豚之氣。亦能泄肺，能補胃，大能療腎。丁香氣味尤佳。震亨曰：口居上，地氣出焉。脾有郁火，溢入肺中，失其清和之意，而濁氣上行，發為口氣。若以丁香治之，是揚湯止沸爾。惟香薷治之甚捷。時珍曰：宋末太醫陳文中，治小兒痘瘡不光澤，不起發，或脹或瀉，或渴或氣促，表裡俱虛之証。並用木香散、異攻散，倍加丁香、官桂。甚者丁香三、五十枚，官桂一、二錢。亦有服之而愈者。此丹溪朱氏所謂立方之時，必運氣在寒水司天之際，又值嚴冬郁遏陽氣，故用大辛熱之劑發之者也。若不分氣血虛實寒熱經絡，一概驟用，其殺人也必矣。葛洪《抱朴子》云：凡百病在目者，以雞舌香、黃連、乳汁煎注之，皆愈。此得辛散苦降養陰之妙。陳承言不可點眼者，蓋不知此理也。",

大明曰︰熱。

元素曰︰陽中微陰。入手太陰、足少陰，通行陽明經。

【紫檀氣味】咸，微寒，無毒。

【主治】消風熱腫毒（弘景）。治中惡鬼氣，殺蟲（藏器）。煎服，止心腹痛，霍亂腎氣痛。水磨，塗外腎並腰腎痛處（大明）。散冷氣，引胃氣上升，進飲食（元素）。噎膈吐食。又面生黑子，每夜以漿水洗拭令赤，磨汁塗之，甚良（時珍）。

摩塗惡毒風毒（《別錄》）。刮末敷金瘡，止血止痛。療淋（弘景）。醋磨，敷一切猝腫（千金）。

【發明】杲曰︰白檀調氣，引芳香之物，上至極高之分。最宜橙、橘之屬，佐以薑、棗，輔以葛根、縮砂、益智、豆蔻，通行陽明之經，在胸膈之上，處咽嗌之間，為理氣要藥。

時珍曰︰《楞嚴經》云︰白旃檀塗身，能除一切熱惱。今西南諸番酋，皆用諸香塗身，取此義也。杜寶《大業錄》云︰隋有壽禪師妙醫術，作五香飲濟人。沉香飲、檀香飲、丁香飲、澤蘭飲、甘松飲，皆以香為主，更加別藥，有味而止渴，兼補益人也。道書檀香謂之浴香，不可燒供上真。

紫檀
時珍曰︰白檀辛溫，氣分之藥也。故能理衛氣而調脾肺，利胸膈。紫檀咸寒，血分之藥也。故能和營氣而消腫毒，治金瘡。",
  "translation": "**[Nomenclature]** Also called Zhāntán [旃檀] (Bencao Gangmu) and Zhēntán [真檀].

Li Shizhen states: Tán [檀] is a 'virtuous wood' [善木], hence its character is formed from the element meaning 'good/virtuous' [善]. Buddhist practitioners call it zhāntán [旃檀], using it for bathing and ablution, implying the removal of impurities [離垢]. Foreigners corrupted this into zhēntán [真檀, 'true sandalwood']. People in Yunnan call purple sandalwood [紫檀] 'superior to agarwood' [勝沉香], which is the same as red sandalwood [赤檀].

**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi [藏器] states: White sandalwood [白檀] comes from Hainan. The tree resembles the sandalwood type.

Su Gong [恭] states: Purple true sandalwood [紫真檀] comes from the Kingdom of Kunlun Panpan [昆侖盤盤國]. Although it does not grow in China proper, it is found widely among people. It is not particularly fragrant.

results.title_inferred: Jiangzhen Xiang (降真香) — Rosewoood/Rosewood Incense: Names, Origins, Properties, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】紫藤香（《綱目》）、雞骨香。

曰︰《仙傳》︰拌和諸香，燒煙直上，感引鶴降。醮星辰，燒此香為第一，度功力極驗。降真之名以此。

時珍曰︰俗呼舶上來者為番降，亦名雞骨，與沉香同名。

【集解】慎微曰︰降真香出黔南。

曰︰生南海山中及大秦國。其香似蘇方木，燒之初不甚香，得諸香和之則特美。入藥以番降紫而潤者為良。

時珍曰︰今廣東、廣西、雲南、漢中、施州、永順、保靖，及占城、安南、暹羅、渤泥、琉球諸地皆有之。朱輔《溪蠻叢笑》云︰雞骨香即降香，本出海南。今溪峒僻處所出者，似是而非，勁瘦不甚香。周達觀《真臘記》云︰降香生叢林中，番人頗費砍斫之功，乃樹心也。其外白皮，濃八、九寸，或五、六寸。焚之氣勁而遠。又嵇含《草木狀》云︰紫藤香，長莖細葉，根極堅實，重重有皮，花白子黑。其莖截置煙炱中，經久成紫香，可降神。按︰嵇氏所說，與前說稍異，豈即朱氏所謂似是而非者乎？抑中國者與番降不同乎？

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】燒之，辟天行時氣，宅舍怪異。小兒帶之，辟邪惡氣（李珣）。療折傷金瘡，止血定痛，消腫生肌（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰降香，唐、宋本草失收。唐慎微始增入之，而不著其功用。今折傷金瘡家多用其節，云可代沒藥、血竭。按︰《名醫錄》云︰周某被海寇刃傷，血出不止，筋如斷，骨如折，用花蕊石散不效。軍士李高用紫金散掩之，血止痛定。明日結痂如鐵，遂愈，且無瘢痕。叩其方，則用紫藤香瓷瓦刮下研末爾。云即降香之最佳者，曾救萬人。羅天益《衛生寶鑑》
results.translation:
**[Nomenclature]** Also known as Zǐténg xiāng [Purple Wisteria Incense] (Bencao Gangmu) and Jīgǔ xiāng [Chicken-bone Incense].

[A source] states: According to the Xiān Chuán [Immortals' Traditions], when this substance is blended with various incenses and burned, the smoke rises straight upward and is said to attract cranes to descend. For offering rites to the stars and constellations, burning this incense is of the first rank, and its efficacy is remarkably proven. The name Jiàngzhēn ["Bringing Down the Immortals/True Ones"] derives from this.

Li Shizhen states: Commonly, that which arrives by ship is called Fān jiàng ["Foreign Jiangzhen"]; it is also called Jīgǔ [Chicken-bone], a name it shares with chen xiang [eaglewood/agarwood].

**[Collected Explanations]** Tang Shenwei states: Jiangzhen xiang originates from Qiannan [southern Guizhou].

[Another source] states: It grows in the mountains of the South Sea region and in the land of Daqin [a name used for the Roman/Byzantine East or Persia]. Its fragrance resembles that of sumu [sappanwood]; when first burned it is not particularly fragrant, but when blended with other incenses it becomes especially fine. For medicinal use, the foreign variety (fān jiàng) that is purple and moist is considered the best.

Li Shizhen states: Today it is found in Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan, Hanzhong, Shizhou, Yongshun, Baojing, and also in the foreign lands of Champa [Zhancheng], Annam [present-day Vietnam], Siam [Thailand], Brunei [Boni], and Ryukyu [Liu Qiu], among other places. Zhu Fu, in his Xī Mán Cóng Xiào, writes: "Chicken-bone incense is jiàng xiāng; it originally comes from Hainan. What is now produced in remote mountain-stream settlements appears to be the same but is not — it is lean and tough and not very fragrant." Zhou Daguan, in his Zhēnlà Jì [Record of Cambodia], writes: "Jiàng xiāng grows in dense forests; the foreign people expend considerable labor in chopping it out, for it is the heartwood of the tree. The outer white bark is eight or nine inches thick, or five or six inches. When burned, its fragrance is strong and far-reaching." Furthermore, Ji Han in his Cǎomù Zhuàng [Account of Plants and Trees] writes: "Zǐténg xiāng has long stems and fine leaves; its roots are extremely firm and solid, with layer upon layer of bark; its flowers are white and its seeds black. When sections of its stem are placed in soot and smoke over a long period, they eventually become purple incense, which can summon spirits [jiàng shén]." Note: What Ji Han describes differs somewhat from the accounts above — might this be the variety Zhu Fu called 'appearing similar but actually different'? Or could it be that the Chinese variety is distinct from the foreign import?

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**[Principal Treatments]** When burned, it wards off epidemic seasonal qi [tiān xíng shí qì] and repels uncanny disturbances in the home. When worn by children, it repels malign and evil qi (Li Xun). It treats fractures and metal-weapon wounds [jīn chuāng], stops bleeding, settles pain, disperses swelling, and promotes the regeneration of flesh (Li Shizhen).

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen states: Jiàng xiāng was omitted from the materia medica of the Tang and Song dynasties. Tang Shenwei was the first to add it, yet he did not record its medicinal functions. Today, practitioners treating fractures and metal-weapon wounds frequently use its nodal sections, saying it can substitute for myrrh [mòyào] and dragon's blood resin [xuèjié]. According to the Míng Yī Lù [Records of Renowned Physicians]: a certain Zhou [surname] was wounded by a blade wielded by sea pirates; the bleeding would not stop, his sinews seemed severed and his bones seemed broken. The application of Huā Ruǐ Shí Sǎn [Ophicalcite Powder] was ineffective. The soldier Li Gao applied Zǐjīn Sǎn [Purple Gold Powder] to cover the wound; the bleeding stopped and the pain subsided. By the next day a crust had formed as hard as iron, and he fully recovered without even a scar. When pressed for the formula, it turned out to consist solely of zǐténg xiāng scraped from a ceramic tile and ground into powder. It is said to be the finest form of jiàng xiāng and has reportedly saved ten thousand people. Luo Tianyi's Wèishēng Bǎo Jiàn [Precious Mirror of Health] … [text ends here; passage is cut off]

entities_mentioned.name: 降真香 (Jiàngzhēn xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dalbergia odorifera T. Chen (Chinese rosewood / Hainan rosewood); possibly also Dalbergia benthamii or related Dalbergia spp. depending on regional source
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 紫藤香 and 雞骨香. Heartwood used medicinally and as incense. The text distinguishes between a domestic Chinese variety and an imported 'foreign' variety (番降), possibly different Dalbergia or Pterocarpus species.

entities_mentioned.name: 雞骨香 (Jīgǔ xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate name for Dalbergia odorifera or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Shared name with a form of chen xiang (agarwood); used here as a synonym for jiangzhen xiang.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘇方木 (Sūfāng mù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caesalpinia sappan L. (Sappanwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced for comparison of fragrance.

entities_mentioned.name: 沉香 (Chén xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria sinensis or Aquilaria spp. (Agarwood/Eaglewood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in passing as sharing the alternate name 雞骨 with jiangzhen xiang.

entities_mentioned.name: 花蕊石散 (Huā Ruǐ Shí Sǎn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophicalcite (metamorphic limestone) powder preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: A hemostatic formula mentioned as having been ineffective in the cited clinical anecdote.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫金散 (Zǐjīn Sǎn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A formula based on jiangzhen xiang (scraped from ceramic and powdered)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the effective hemostatic and wound-healing remedy in the anecdote; its active ingredient is identified as zǐténg xiāng (jiangzhen xiang).

entities_mentioned.name: 沒藥 (Mòyào)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha (Myrrh)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a substance that jiangzhen xiang can replace in wound treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: 血竭 (Xuèjié)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daemonorops draco or Dracaena spp. (Dragon's blood resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as another substance that jiangzhen xiang can replace in wound treatment.

results.translator_notes:
1. The attribution tag '曰︰' (lit. '[Someone] said') appears without a named speaker in two places; these likely refer to lost or partially attributed sources now embedded in the text — possibly Li Xun (李珣) for the first instance (consistent with the 'Li Xun' attribution in the Zhìliáo section) and an unnamed pharmacopoeial source for the second. The cleaned text restores '李珣' and '[某]' where context makes the attribution clear.
2. The passage is abruptly cut off mid-sentence at 'Luo Tianyi's Weisheng Baojian'; the source text is incomplete at this segment boundary.
3. 'Daqin' (大秦國) in Tang and Song-era texts typically refers to the Byzantine Empire or the broader Roman/Persian Near East; its precise meaning here is uncertain but indicates a distant western origin.
4. The character rendered '李 ' in the original OCR for the Zhìliáo attribution has been silently corrected to '李珣' (Li Xun), a known pharmacologist whose Hǎiyào Běncǎo is a recognized source for this entry.
5. '周某' — the surname Zhou is clear but the given name is lost in the original or OCR; rendered as 'a certain Zhou' in translation.

results.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其木理多紋章，故謂之樟。

【集解】藏器曰：江東造船多用樟木。縣名豫章，因木得名。

時珍曰：西南處處山谷有之。木高丈餘。小葉似楠而尖長，背有黃赤茸毛，四時不凋。夏開細花，結小子。木大者數抱，肌理細而錯縱有紋，宜於雕刻，氣甚芬烈。豫、章乃二木名，一類二種也。豫即釣樟，見下條。

樟材

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】惡氣中惡，心腹痛鬼疰，霍亂腹脹，宿食不消，常吐酸臭水，酒煮服，無藥處用之。煎湯，浴腳氣、疥癬風癢。作履，除腳氣。

【發明】時珍曰：霍亂及乾霍亂須吐者，以樟木屑煎濃汁吐之，甚良。又中惡死者，以樟木燒煙熏之，待蘇乃用藥。此物辛烈香竄，能去濕氣、辟邪惡故也。

風疰鬼邪（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen states: The grain of this wood displays many intricate patterns [wen zhang 紋章], and thus it is called zhang [camphor].

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: In Jiangdong [eastern China], ships are commonly built using camphor wood. The prefecture name Yuzhang [modern Jiangxi region] was derived from this tree.

Li Shizhen states: This tree is found throughout the mountain valleys of the southwest. The trunk grows to over a zhang in height [approx. 3+ metres]. Its small leaves resemble those of the nan tree [Phoebe/Machilus sp.] but are more pointed and elongated, with yellow-reddish downy hairs on the underside, and they do not fall in any of the four seasons. In summer it produces delicate flowers and sets small fruit. Large specimens require several arm-spans to embrace. The grain is fine yet interlocking and patterned, making it well-suited for carving; the fragrance is exceedingly powerful and piercing. Yu and Zhang are in fact names for two trees — two varieties of one category. Yu refers to the 'fishing camphor' [Diao Zhang, Lindera aggregate or related species], described in the following entry.

**Camphor Timber**

**Flavor and Nature**
Acrid [xin], warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
- Malign qi and being struck by malign influences [zhong e]; pain in the chest and abdomen; ghost-infusion illness [gui zhu, a condition of chronic wandering pain attributed to supernatural causes]
- Cholera [huo luan] with abdominal distension
- Undigested retained food, with habitual vomiting of sour, foul-smelling fluid — decoct in wine and take orally; to be used where no other medicine is available
- Decoct into a wash-bath to treat leg-qi [jiao qi, beriberi/lower limb swelling], scabies, ringworm, and wind-itch
- Made into footwear to relieve leg-qi
- Wind-infusion and ghost-evil [feng zhu gui xie] (according to Li Shizhen)

**Elaboration**
Li Shizhen states: For cholera and dry cholera where vomiting must be induced, decocting camphor wood shavings into a strong concentrated liquid and using it to provoke vomiting is highly effective. Furthermore, for those who have collapsed from being struck by malign influences, burn camphor wood to produce smoke and fumigate the patient; once they revive, administer medicines. This substance is acrid, intensely aromatic, and penetrating in nature, and is therefore capable of dispelling dampness and warding off evil and malign influences.

entities_mentioned.name: 樟 (Zhang / Camphor Tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum camphora (L.) J.Presl — camphor tree
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most commercially and medicinally important trees of East Asia; source of natural camphor. The description of evergreen leaves with downy undersides, aromatic wood with complex grain, and the geographic distribution all match C. camphora precisely.

entities_mentioned.name: 楠 (Nan tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phoebe or Machilus spp. (family Lauraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of camphor; also a member of Lauraceae, consistent with the comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 釣樟 (Diao Zhang / Fishing Camphor)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lindera aggregate or Cinnamomum japonicum — possibly Lindera sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen identifies this as 'Yu' (豫), described as related to but distinct from Zhang (camphor), and cross-references it to the following entry in the text.

entities_mentioned.name: 鬼疰 (Gui Zhu / Ghost Infusion Illness)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely encompasses tuberculosis or other chronic wasting/infectious conditions in modern terms
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical category referring to chronic, transmissible, or wandering pain and illness attributed to malign supernatural agents.

entities_mentioned.name: 脚氣 (Jiao Qi / Leg Qi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beriberi (thiamine deficiency) or peripheral oedema of the lower limbs
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical term for a condition involving swelling, weakness, or numbness of the legs; in some contexts corresponds to beriberi.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text contains one OCR corruption: 'KT' in '江東KT船多用樟木' is clearly a garbled character for 造 (zào), meaning 'to build/construct'; corrected silently to 造 in the cleaned text. 2. The 【主治】section and 【發明】section each appear twice in the source due to apparent copy-duplication in the original OCR; the duplicated 【發明】block has been merged into a single instance in the cleaned text. 3. The phrase '作履' (make into footwear) in the original appears to be truncated mid-sentence in the source; the continuation '除腳氣' (removes leg-qi) has been inferred from context and classical parallel texts and added in brackets in the cleaned text. 4. 'Yuzhang' (豫章) was a historical commandery name corresponding roughly to modern Jiangxi Province, and the etymological claim linking the name to the camphor tree is a traditional account.

results.original_text:
【釋名】烏樟（弘景）、[KT]

時珍曰︰樟有大、小二種，紫、淡二色。此即樟之小者。按︰鄭樵《通志》云︰釣樟亦樟之類，即《爾雅》所謂「[KT]，無疵」是也。又相如賦云︰[KT]、楠、豫、章。顏師古注云︰豫即枕木，章即樟木。二木生至七年，乃可分別。觀此，則豫即《別錄》所謂釣樟者也。根似烏藥香，故又名烏樟。

【集解】弘景曰︰釣樟出桂陽、邵陵諸處，亦呼作烏樟。恭曰︰生郴州山谷。樹高丈餘。葉似楠葉而尖長，背有赤毛，若枇杷葉上毛。八月、九月採根皮，晒乾。

炳曰︰根似烏藥香。

藏器曰︰枕生南海山谷。作[KT]船，次於樟木。

根皮

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】金瘡止血，刮屑敷之，甚驗（《別錄》）。磨服，治霍亂（蕭炳）。治奔豚腳氣水腫，煎湯服。亦可浴瘡痍疥癬風瘙，並研末敷之（大明）。

莖葉
置門上，辟天行時氣（蕭炳）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Also called Wu Zhang ["black camphor"] (Tao Hongjing); [additional name illegible: likely a character from the Erya].

Li Shizhen states: There are two varieties of zhang [camphor tree] — large and small — and two colours — purple and pale. This plant is the smaller variety. According to Zheng Qiao's *Tongzhi*: Diao Zhang belongs to the camphor family and is the plant referred to in the *Erya* as "[ILLEGIBLE character], without blemish." Furthermore, in Sima Xiangru's rhapsody, the trees [ILLEGIBLE character], nan [Phoebe nanmu], yu, and zhang are listed together. Yan Shigu's commentary states: yu is the zhen [pillow] wood, and zhang is the camphor wood. The two trees cannot be distinguished until they have grown for seven years. From this it is clear that yu as described in the *Bielu* [Miscellaneous Records] is the same as Diao Zhang. Its root has an aroma resembling that of wu yao [Lindera aggregate], hence it is also called Wu Zhang ["black camphor"].

**Collected Explanations**
Tao Hongjing states: Diao Zhang grows in Guiyang, Shaoling, and surrounding areas; it is also called Wu Zhang.
Su Gong states: It grows in the mountain valleys of Chenzhou. The tree stands over one zhang [~3 m] tall. Its leaves resemble those of nan [Phoebe] but are more pointed and elongated, with reddish hairs on the undersides, like the hairs on the upper surface of loquat leaves. The root bark is collected in the eighth and ninth months and dried in the sun.

Xiao Bing states: The root has the fragrance of wu yao.

Chen Cangqi states: The zhen [pillow] tree grows in the mountain valleys of the South Seas. It is used to make [ILLEGIBLE: a type of] boats, being second in quality to camphor wood.

---
**Root Bark**

*Qi and Flavour:* Acrid [xin], warm [wen], non-toxic.

*Indications and Treatments:*
- For metal-weapon wounds [jin chuang] with bleeding: scrape off shavings and apply topically — extremely effective. (*Bielu*)
- Ground and taken orally, treats sudden turmoil illness [huo luan / acute cholera-like disorder]. (Xiao Bing)
- Treats running piglet disorder [ben tun], leg qi [jiao qi / beriberi], and water swelling [shui zhong / edema]: decoct and take orally. May also be used as a bath for sores, scabies, tinea, and wind-itch [feng sao]; alternatively grind into powder and apply topically. (Da Ming)

---
**Stems and Leaves**
Placed above the door, they ward off epidemic seasonal qi [tian xing shi qi / airborne pestilential influences]. (Xiao Bing)

entities_mentioned.name: 釣樟 (Diao Zhang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lindera aggregate (wu yao) relative, possibly Cinnamomum japonicum or Lindera umbellata; some authorities equate it with Lindera glauca or a related Lauraceae shrub
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the 'small' camphor; root aroma likened to wu yao (Lindera aggregate). The text's identification with the classical 'yu' tree adds ambiguity. Likely a member of Lauraceae.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏藥 (Wu Yao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lindera aggregata (Sims) Kosterm.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative aromatic reference for the root of Diao Zhang.

entities_mentioned.name: 楠 (Nan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phoebe nanmu (Oliv.) Gamble or related Phoebe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced for leaf comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 枇杷 (Pi Pa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriobotrya japonica (Thunb.) Lindl. — loquat
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced for comparison of leaf-hair texture.

entities_mentioned.name: 奔豚 (Ben Tun)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical TCM condition: 'running piglet disorder,' characterised by sensation of qi rushing upward from abdomen to throat. Treated here with decoction of root bark.

entities_mentioned.name: 腳氣 (Jiao Qi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beriberi / peripheral neuropathy with edema, often associated with thiamine deficiency in historical context
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical TCM term; root bark decoction prescribed.

results.translator_notes:
1. Multiple characters are marked [KT] in the source, indicating OCR failures or missing glyphs; these likely include a rare character from the Erya used as an alternate name for the tree, and possibly a tree name in the Sima Xiangru rhapsody listing. They have been rendered as [ILLEGIBLE] with context in the translation.
2. The identification of 'yu' (豫/枕) with Diao Zhang is the author's own philological argument, not universally accepted.
3. 'Tian xing shi qi' (天行時氣) rendered as 'epidemic seasonal qi' — a classical concept referring to contagious seasonal pestilential disease, not a specific modern diagnosis.
4. Preparation methods extracted: (a) topical application of scraped bark shavings for wounds; (b) ground/powdered bark taken orally for huo luan; (c) decoction for internal conditions; (d) bath preparation for skin conditions; (e) stems/leaves placed at doorway as a prophylactic against epidemic illness.

results.title_inferred: Huai Xiang / Douloupoxiang – An Aromatic Wood and Its Root
results.original_text:
【釋名】兜婁婆香。

【集解】時珍曰︰香，江淮、湖嶺山中有之。木大者近葉青而長，有鋸齒，狀如小薊葉而香，對節生。其根狀如枸杞根而大，煨之甚香。《楞嚴經》云︰壇前

根

【氣味】苦，澀，平，無毒。

【主治】頭癤腫毒。碾末，麻脂調塗，七日腐落（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Also called Douloupoxiang [兜婁婆香; a transliteration of a Sanskrit term, likely *dhūpa* or a related aromatic].

**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: This fragrant plant is found in the mountains of Jianghuai [the Yangtze–Huai River region] and Huling [Hunan/Lingnan area]. The larger trees have leaves that are greenish and elongated near the tips, with serrated edges, resembling the leaves of small thistle [小薊, *Cephalanoplos segetum*] and are fragrant; the leaves grow in opposite pairs at the nodes. The root resembles the root of wolfberry [枸杞, *Lycium chinense*] but is larger; when roasted over embers it is very fragrant. The *Śūraṃgama Sūtra* [《楞嚴經》] states: 'Before the altar—' [ILLEGIBLE: text appears truncated here; likely a quotation about burning this incense before a ritual altar].

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, astringent, neutral [平], non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:** Furuncles and toxic swellings of the head. Grind to a powder, mix with hemp oil [麻脂] and apply topically; within seven days the necrotic tissue will slough off. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 櫰香 (Huai Xiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a species of aromatic shrub or tree in the Lamiaceae or Lauraceae family; some scholars associate 兜婁婆香 with a Sanskrit incense plant, potentially *Valeriana jatamansi* or a related aromatic taxon
entities_mentioned.notes: The Sanskrit-derived alternate name 兜婁婆香 (Douloupoxiang) suggests a Buddhist ritual incense plant imported or naturalized in China; the botanical description (serrated leaves like small thistle, opposite nodes, fragrant root) does not match a single well-established modern species with confidence.

entities_mentioned.name: 小薊 (Xiao Ji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cephalanoplos segetum (field thistle) or Cirsium setosum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a comparative descriptor for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞 (Gou Qi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense or Lycium barbarum (wolfberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a comparative descriptor for root morphology.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻脂 (Ma Zhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hemp seed oil (from Cannabis sativa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the vehicle/excipient for the topical preparation.

results.translator_notes: The 【集解】section appears to be truncated or damaged at the point of the Śūraṃgama Sūtra quotation—the text breaks off after '壇前' ('before the altar') with no closing passage. This is flagged as likely an OCR or manuscript lacuna. The entry heading 櫰香 may itself be a partial or corrupted character form; Li Shizhen's description strongly frames this as an aromatic incense wood with ritual Buddhist associations. The medical use (topical application for head furuncles) is attributed to Li Shizhen himself, indicating original or compiled clinical observation rather than citation of an earlier source.

results.title_inferred: Bilì Xiāng (必栗香) — A Fragrant Mountain Wood
results.original_text:
【釋名】花木香、詹香。

【集解】藏器曰︰必栗香生高山中。葉如老椿，搗置上流，魚悉暴腮而死。木白魚不損書也。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】鬼疰心氣，斷一切惡氣，煮汁服之。燒為香，殺蟲、魚（藏器）。
results.translation:
【Names】 Also known as Huā Mù Xiāng [Flower Wood Fragrance] and Zhān Xiāng [Zhān Fragrance].

【Collected Explanations】 [Chen] Cangqi states: Bìlì Xiāng grows in high mountains. Its leaves resemble those of the aged Chinese toon [Toona sinensis]. When pounded and placed in a stream above [a pool of fish], all the fish expose their gills and die. The wood [or: the white of the wood] does not harm fish used as bookmarks [i.e., it repels bookworms without damaging stored texts].

【Qi and Flavor】 Acrid [pungent], warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】 [It treats] ghost-infixation [guì zhù, a condition of chronic wasting illness attributed to malevolent spirits] and heart-qi [disturbances]; it cuts off all malevolent qi — decoct and drink the juice. When burned as incense, it kills insects and fish. (Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 必栗香 (Bìlì Xiāng)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 花木香 (Huā Mù Xiāng) and 詹香 (Zhān Xiāng). Described as a high-mountain tree with leaves resembling aged Chinese toon (Toona sinensis) and notable ichthyotoxic (fish-poisoning) properties. No confident modern botanical identification has been established; possibly a species in Meliaceae or a related family given the toon-leaf comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 老椿 (Aged Chinese Toon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Toona sinensis (Meliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological reference for leaf shape comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 鬼疰 (Guì Zhù)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical concept referring to a chronic, contagious wasting condition believed to be caused by malevolent or ghostly influences. Roughly analogous to descriptions of tuberculosis or other debilitating diseases in some scholarly interpretations.

results.translator_notes: The phrase '木白魚不損書也' is somewhat ambiguous. '白魚' (báiyú) in the context of books typically refers to silverfish (Lepisma saccharina), the paper-eating insect. The sentence most likely means: 'The wood repels silverfish without damaging books,' indicating an anti-bookworm property. Alternatively, '木白' could refer to the sapwood. The ichthyotoxic (fish-killing) use of the pounded leaves is consistent with rotenone-bearing or saponin-bearing plants traditionally used as fish poisons. Source attribution throughout is to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), author of the Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺, c. 739 CE).

results.title_inferred: Sweetgum Resin (Fēng Xiāng Zhī) — Entry from Chapter Wood I
results.original_text:
【釋名】白膠香。

時珍曰︰楓樹枝弱善搖，故字從風。俗呼香楓。《金光明經》謂其香為須薩折羅婆香。

頌曰︰《爾雅》謂楓為攤，言風至則攤而鳴也。《梵書》謂之薩折羅婆香。

【集解】恭曰︰楓香脂，所在大山中皆有之。

頌曰︰今南方及關陝甚多。樹甚高碩，似白楊。葉圓而作歧，有三角而香。二月有花，白色。乃連著實，大如鴨卵。八月、九月熟時，曝乾可燒。《南方草木狀》云︰楓實惟九真有楓木，南中有楓子鬼。木之老者為人形，亦呼為靈楓，蓋瘤癭也。至今越巫可致靈異。

為楓木之林。《爾雅》注云︰其脂入地，千年為琥珀。

其木甚堅，有赤有白，白者細膩。其實成球，有否？孫炎《爾雅正義》云︰楓子鬼乃荀伯子《臨海記》所記。《宋齊丘化書》云︰老楓化為羽人。

【氣味】辛、苦，平，無毒。
辛，平，有小毒（蘇恭）。
有毒，食之令人笑不止，地漿解之。

【主治】癮疹風癢浮腫，煮水浴之。又主齒痛（《唐本》）。一切癰疽瘡疥，金瘡吐衄咯血，活血生肌，止痛解毒。燒過揩牙，永無牙疾（時珍）。水腫，下水氣，煮汁用之（蘇恭）。煎飲，止水痢為最（藏器）。止霍亂，刺風冷風，煎湯浴之。癰疽已成，擂酒飲，以滓貼之（時珍）。

【發明】震亨曰︰楓香屬金，有水與火。其性疏通故木易有蟲穴，為外科要藥。近世不知，誤以松脂之清瑩者為之，甚謬。

宗奭曰︰楓香、松脂，皆可亂乳香。但楓香微白黃色，燒之可見真偽。

時珍曰︰楓香、松脂皆可亂乳香，其功雖次於乳香，而亦仿佛不遠。
results.translation:
**[Nomenclature]** Also known as Báijiāo Xiāng [White Gum Resin].

Li Shizhen states: The branches of the fēng [sweetgum] tree are slender and readily sway, hence the character is written with the element for 'wind' [風]. It is commonly called Xiāng Fēng [Fragrant Maple/Sweetgum]. The *Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra* [Golden Light Sutra] refers to its fragrance as 'Susajjara-bha incense.'

Su Song states: The *Erya* calls the fēng tree [a character indicating swaying], signifying that when the wind comes it sways and makes a sound. The Sanskrit texts call it 'Sajjara-bha incense.'

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Gong states: Sweetgum resin [Fēng xiāng zhī] is found throughout the great mountains wherever the tree grows.

Su Song states: Today it is very abundant in the southern regions and in the Guān and Shǎn areas. The tree is very tall and large, resembling the white poplar [Populus alba]. Its leaves are rounded and lobed, with three-pointed angles, and are fragrant. In the second month it produces white flowers. The fruits are borne in clusters, as large as a duck's egg. When they ripen in the eighth or ninth month, they may be sun-dried and burned as incense. The *Nanfang Caomu Zhuang* [Accounts of Plants of the Southern Regions] states: As for the fruit of the sweetgum, only in Jiūzhēn [northern Vietnam] does the sweetgum grow; in the southern regions there are 'fēng-child spirits' [fēngzǐ guǐ]. Very old trees take on human forms and are also called 'spirit sweetgums' [líng fēng]—these are in fact tumorous galls [liú yǐng]. To this day, the shamans of Yuè can invoke strange powers from them.

[A passage describes] a forest of sweetgum trees. The commentary to the *Erya* states: When the resin enters the ground, after a thousand years it becomes amber [hǔpò].

The wood is very hard; there are red and white varieties, with the white being finer-grained. The fruit forms a sphere. Sun Yan's *Erya Zhengyi* [Correct Meaning of the Erya] states that the 'fēng-child spirit' is the same entity recorded by Xún Bózi in the *Linhai Ji* [Records of Linhai]. The *Huashu* [Book of Transformation] attributed to Song Qi-qiu states: An aged sweetgum transforms into a feathered immortal [yǔrén].

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid and bitter; balanced [píng]; non-toxic.
Acrid; balanced; slightly toxic (Su Gong).
Toxic: ingesting it causes uncontrollable laughter; this is antidoted by earth-wash [dì jiāng, a preparation of water stirred with earth].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Urticaria [yǐnzhěn], wind-itching [fēng yǎng], and edema [fú zhǒng]: boil in water and use as a bath. Also indicated for toothache. (*Tangben* [Tang Materia Medica])
- All manner of abscesses [yōng jū], sores and scabies [chuāng jiè], metal-wound bleeding [jīn chuāng], vomiting of blood, nosebleed, and coughing of blood: invigorates the blood, promotes tissue regeneration [shēng jī], relieves pain, and resolves toxins. Burning the resin and rubbing the teeth with the ash will permanently prevent dental disease. (Li Shizhen)
- Edema [shuǐ zhǒng], to discharge water-qi [xià shuǐ qì]: cook the juice and use it internally. (Su Gong)
- Decoct and drink: most effective for stopping watery dysentery [shuǐ lì]. (Zang Qi [Chen Cangqi])
- Stops cholera [huòluàn]; for piercing wind [cì fēng] and cold wind [lěng fēng]: decoct and use as a bath.
- For established abscess [yōng jū yǐ chéng]: grind into liquor and drink; apply the dregs topically as a poultice. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]** Zhu Zhenheng states: Sweetgum resin belongs to the Metal phase and partakes of both Water and Fire. Its nature is dispersing and penetrating, which is why the wood is easily riddled with insect holes; it is an important medicine for external [surgical] medicine [wàikē]. In recent times this has not been understood, and people mistakenly substitute the clear, translucent variety of pine resin for it — a serious error.

Kou Zongshi states: Sweetgum resin and pine resin can both be used to adulterate frankincense [rǔ xiāng]. However, sweetgum resin is slightly whitish-yellow in color; burning it will reveal whether it is genuine or not.

Li Shizhen states: Both sweetgum resin and pine resin can be used to adulterate frankincense. Although their efficacy is somewhat inferior to frankincense, it is not far removed.

entities_mentioned.name: 楓香脂 (Fēng xiāng zhī) / 白膠香 (Báijiāo xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquidambar formosana resin (Chinese sweetgum / Formosan sweetgum); possibly also Liquidambar orientalis
entities_mentioned.notes: The resin of Liquidambar formosana, collected from incisions in the bark. Known in commerce as storax or sweetgum resin. The text distinguishes it clearly from pine resin and frankincense. The description of leaves, fruit size (like a duck's egg), and ball-shaped seed capsule is consistent with Liquidambar spp.

entities_mentioned.name: 琥珀 (hǔpò)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amber (fossilized tree resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: The text repeats the ancient Chinese belief, also found in the Erya commentary, that sweetgum resin buried for a thousand years becomes amber. This is historically significant though botanically inaccurate (true amber derives from extinct conifers).

entities_mentioned.name: 松脂 (sōng zhī)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pine resin (Pinus spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a substance that can adulterate both sweetgum resin and frankincense; visually distinguishable by its clearer, more transparent appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: 乳香 (rǔ xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Frankincense (Boswellia spp. resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative standard; sweetgum resin and pine resin are noted as adulterants for it.

entities_mentioned.name: 地漿 (dì jiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Earth-wash / clay water antidote preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: A traditional antidote preparation made by stirring water with yellow earth (clay) and letting it settle; the clear liquid was consumed to counteract toxins.

entities_mentioned.name: 楓子鬼 (fēngzǐ guǐ) / 靈楓 (líng fēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tumorous galls or burls on Liquidambar formosana
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as old trees developing human-like forms, interpreted by Li Shizhen as 瘤癭 (liú yǐng), i.e., tumorous galls or burls. The folkloric belief in 'sweetgum child spirits' (fēngzǐ guǐ) is noted as a Yue-region shamanic tradition.

results.translator_notes:
1. Several characters in the original appear to have been damaged or incompletely captured by OCR. The character used in the Erya reference for the swaying/sound of the tree (rendered as spaces or placeholders in the source) has been indicated with a bracketed note. The passage referencing 孫炎《爾雅正義》and《宋齊丘化書》appears partially garbled in the source (with a fragment 'having否？'); this has been reconstructed from context to the best extent possible, and the lacuna is noted.
2. The 'Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra' (金光明經) transliteration of the incense name (須薩折羅婆 / 薩折羅婆) corresponds to a Sanskrit term for a resinous incense; the exact Sanskrit original is uncertain.
3. The section header 菌 appearing mid-text before 【發明】 appears to be a stray OCR artifact or typographic error in the source and has been omitted from the cleaned text as it is unintelligible in context.
4. Attribution markers in brackets (e.g., Su Gong, Su Song, Zhu Zhenheng, Kou Zongshi, Li Shizhen) have been preserved and clarified in the translation.
5. The medical conditions treated are: urticaria, wind-itching, edema, toothache, abscesses, sores, scabies, hemorrhagic wounds, hemoptysis, epistaxis, hematemesis, watery dysentery, cholera, and various 'wind' conditions — prepared as baths, decoctions, topical poultices, or tooth-powder.

宗奭曰︰薰陸即乳香，為其垂滴如乳頭也。熔塌在地者為塌香，皆一也。

時珍曰︰佛書謂之天澤香，言其潤澤也。又謂之多伽羅香，又曰杜嚕香。李 言薰陸是樹皮，乳是樹脂。陳藏器言乳是薰陸之類。寇宗奭言是一物。

陳承言薰陸是總名，乳是薰陸之乳頭也。今考《香譜》言乳有十餘品，則乳乃熏陸中似乳頭之一品爾。陳承之說為近理。二物原附沉香下，宋《嘉 本草》分出二條，今據諸說，合並為一。

【集解】恭曰︰薰陸香形似白膠香，出天竺者色白，出單于者夾綠

曰︰按︰《廣志》云︰薰陸香是樹皮鱗甲也，紫赤如櫻桃，透明者為上。

云︰薰陸出大秦國。在海邊有大樹，枝葉正如古松，生於膠。夷人采取賣與商賈，無賈則自食之。

之，謂之西香，南番者更佳，即乳香

承曰︰西出天竺，南出波斯等國。西者色黃白，南者色紫赤。日久重疊者，不成乳頭，雜以沙石。其成乳者，乃新出未雜沙石者也。薰陸是總名，乳是薰陸之乳頭也。今松脂、楓脂中，亦有此狀者甚多。

史》言乳香有一松。以斤斫樹，脂溢於外，結而成香，聚而成塊。上品為揀香，圓大如乳頭，透明，俗呼滴乳。次曰明乳，其色亞於揀香。又次為瓶香，以瓶收者。

時珍曰：佛書謂之天澤香，言其潤澤也。又謂之多伽羅香，又曰杜嚕香。李珣言薰陸是樹皮，乳是樹脂。陳藏器言乳是薰陸之類。寇宗奭言是一物。陳承言薰陸是總名，乳是薰陸之乳頭也。今考《香譜》言乳有十餘品，則乳乃薰陸中似乳頭之一品爾。陳承之說為近理。二物原附沉香下，宋《嘉祐本草》分出二條，今據諸說，合並為一。

【集解】恭曰：薰陸香形似白膠香，出天竺者色白，出單于者夾綠色。

珣曰：按《廣志》云：薰陸香是樹皮鱗甲也，紫赤如櫻桃，透明者為上。

藏器云：薰陸出大秦國。在海邊有大樹，枝葉正如古松，生於膠。夷人采取賣與商賈，無賈則自食之。

宗奭曰：西出天竺，謂之西香，南番者更佳，即乳香也。

承曰：西出天竺，南出波斯等國。西者色黃白，南者色紫赤。日久重疊者，不成乳頭，雜以沙石。其成乳者，乃新出未雜沙石者也。薰陸是總名，乳是薰陸之乳頭也。今松脂、楓脂中，亦有此狀者甚多。

時珍曰：《宋史》言乳香有一松。以斤斫樹，脂溢於外，結而成香，聚而成塊。上品為揀香，圓大如乳頭，透明，俗呼滴乳。次曰明乳，其色亞於揀香。又次為瓶香，以瓶收者。又次曰袋香，言收時只置袋中。次為乳塌，雜沙石者。次為黑塌，色黑。次為水濕塌，水漬色敗氣變者。次為斫削，雜碎不堪。次為纏末，播揚為塵者。觀此則乳有自流出者，有斫樹溢出者。諸說皆言其樹類松。寇氏言類棠梨，恐亦傳聞，當從前說。道書乳香、檀香謂之浴香，不可燒祀上真。

【氣味】微溫，無毒。

大明曰：乳香：辛，熱，微毒。元素曰：苦、辛，純陽。

震亨曰：善竄，入手少陰經。

【主治】薰陸：主風水毒腫，去惡氣伏尸，癮疹癢毒。乳香同功（《別錄》）。乳香：治耳聾，中風口噤不語，婦人血氣，止大腸泄瀉，療諸瘡，令內消，能發酒，理風冷（藏器）。

下氣益精，補腰膝，治腎氣，止霍亂，沖惡中邪氣，心腹痛疰氣。煎膏，治不眠（之才）。補腎，定諸經之痛（元素）。仙方用以辟谷（李珣）。消癰疽諸毒，托裡護心，活血定痛伸筋，治婦人難產折傷（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：乳香香竄，能入心經，活血定痛，故為癰疽瘡瘍、心腹痛要藥。《素問》云諸痛癢瘡瘍皆屬心火是矣。產科諸方多用之，亦取其活血之功爾。陳自明《婦人良方》云：知蘄州施少卿，得神寢丸方於蘄州徐太丞，云婦人臨產月服之，令胎滑易生，極有效驗。用通明乳香半兩，枳殼一兩，為末，煉蜜丸梧桐子大，每空心酒服三十丸。李嗣立治癰疽初起，內托護心散，云：香徹瘡孔中，能使毒氣外出，不致內攻也。方見穀部綠豆下。按：葛洪《抱朴子》云：浮炎洲在南海中，出薰陸香，乃樹有傷穿，木膠流墮。夷人采之，恆患獸啖之。此獸斫刺不死，以杖打之皮不傷，而骨碎乃死。觀此，則乳香之治折傷，雖能活血止痛，亦其性然也。楊清叟……",
